item.title: Re: Good
item.content:encoded:
Why is it keter? Keter means it's incredibly difficult or impossible to contain. Do these viruses, bacteria, and prions have some type of sentience? How do they work together to cause this infection?

So, I'm obviously not the author, but I see it as being a combination of all the bacteria, viruses and prions that together become greater than the sum of their parts. To effectively 'contain' this, you'd need to contain all of at least one of those microorganisms. Not easy, hence keter.

item.title: Good
item.content:encoded:
I gave you +1, but there's an important, unanswered question: Why is it keter? Keter means it's incredibly difficult or impossible to contain. Do these viruses, bacteria, and prions have some type of sentience? How do they work together to cause this infection?

Also, the set-up explaining -1,-2, and -3 was a little confusing to me. I figured it out after a second run-through; the confusion is probably just me.

I loved the log you added. I'm really interested in the implications of an active eye operating separate from it's host body. Thank you for sharing this article! I hope you're proud of it! 😊

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scrapie is a prion disease of sheep that can be passed between animals by incidental contact, and sheep can also be infected by grazing in pastures where infected sheep were kept for years afterward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention, all pirons that I've heard of are spread through consumption of infected tissue, and unless all of these pirons can be acquired through eating a non-human animal, I doubt that there would be many cases of this SCP (that is, unless there's cannibals out there that get infected).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, so I noticed while searching for 1802 that 1801 popped up. 1801-3 was said as 1802-3. Went ahead and fixed it, hope that's okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sharing is caring!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The multi-agent vector seems unneccessarily complicated to me. It feels like a wrinkle tacked on specifically to make the disease harder to contain. But wouldn't it actually make it easier to contain? With a standard single vector infection you must contain that infectious agent to contain that disease, which can be practically impossible. With a multi-part vector, you only have to contain one part to contain the whole disease. More options drastically improves the odds of one of the vectors being manageable. Or you could just focus on making sure the various at-risk populations for each vector don't overlap. You don't have to wipe-out virus A world-wide, just in the region where bacteria B thrives and prion C propagates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason I thought the last part was going to be something like, the last infected person was somehow pregnant and the growth was somehow the offspring regrowing in the new person. And yet, I wasn't disappointed by the fact I was wrong.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's like… An early stage after only one or two people get absorbed tumor-style. Eventually you end up with Mantorok, the corpse-god

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can actually imagine this, y'know. God do I hate my brain sometimes.
I'm actually wondering whether this infection could build-up, if left long enough.
I mean, what would happen if it consumed 2's throat, and while this may affect 3's, it may act as a suitable replacement, and the two would eventually merge, including the brains, to form a multi-brained and multi-organ'd pile of living flesh, which in turn was capable of infecting more people, which then would also add to it, and after a while you'd end up with something like looks like this.
So much potential. +1. Very original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. My only issue is that the teratoma is speaking at the end, but there is no mention of a lung. It would be more logical if it was mouthing the words instead of actually speaking,

item.title: Tried to clean up the wall of text feel.
item.content:encoded: And some other tweaks here and there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a tad wordy, but it's really creepy and clever. The article just needs some cleaning up is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I wanted to pass on this, but then I read that last bit. Yikes. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had it hit me today, it's something with Wikidot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's with all the double posts recently? I've seen, like, five just in the past week.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it wasn't starting to show sentience, I might agree with you.

Also, there's a problem that it isn't just one organism, but a collection of otherwise mundane bacteria and virus. Containing this requires containing all its components, or preventing them from ganging up in the wild, and we don't know how to do that. And because its initial stage is asymptomatic, SCP-1801-1 could be passing on the pieces-parts without suffering fatal symptoms until it passed on full blown SCP-1801. So SCP-1801-1 might not casually give you SCP-1801-1, but could pass on something like Ebola or Pneumonic Plague.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While SCP-1801 itself has only been seen to transmit with direct blood-to-blood or sexual contact

I'm not seeing how this is a Keter level threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was very good, the incident report was a it OOT, but to the extent that I want to see more regarding it, rather than see it removed, That's just my opinion though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm now reminded of a line that I mis-read and wanted to make into a story, but never got it to work.
I had read "I need your help to flesh and expand this," as "I need your help and flesh to expand this."
I now don't have to finish writing that story because god dammit this is better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must admit, trying to read through this was a little rough (my scientific vernacular is lacking) but it seems well done enough. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is creepy as hell. My only quibble is that you refer to the subcomponents of the SCP before they are defined in a way that is disorienting. Reorganizing for clarity would help.

The incident report is a tiny bit over the top, but i wouldn't excise it completely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ack, browser crash = double post, sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let me know if the incident report goes over the top.
